front: Novel: subtitled 'For Madmen Only'; the protagonist reads a treatise calling him a 'suicide' who may become an 'immortal'
Novel: the saxophonist Pablo takes the protagonist to the Magic Theater; Hermine is killed there
Novel: the protagonist steps through doors marked 'ALL GIRLS ARE YOURS' and 'HOW ONE KILLS FOR LOVE' in a hall of mirrors
Novel: the protagonist offends a right-wing nationalist professor by insulting a portrait of Goethe
Novel: Mozart hands the protagonist a radio that plays the music of Handel; elsewhere, Brahms and Wagner are seen 'striving for redemption'
Novel: a potted araucaria standing outside the protagonist's apartment contrasts with his turbulent lifestyle
Novel: the protagonist abandons his home with Govinda and learns wisdom from the ferryman Vasudeva by listening to the river
Novel: the protagonist works with the merchant Kamaswami and loves Kamala, who later dies from a snakebite
Novel: the protagonist dreams of a dead songbird in a gold cage belonging to his lover
Novel: at the novel's end, the protagonist envisions a river of faces after kissing the forehead of his friend, who wears a much-admired secret smile
Novel: Joseph Knecht becomes 'Magister Ludi' in the province of Castalia; the title activity combines math and music
Novel: Plinio Designori criticizes the title activity's mental life without real-world action; his son Tito watches the protagonist drown
Novel: the game's symbolic language was developed by Joculator Basiliensis, who reduced math and music to a common denominator
Novel: boarding schools are set up to overthrow the pop-history lectures of the 'Age of the Feuilleton'
Novel: the protagonist visits the monastery Mariafels, where he meets Father Jacobus (representing Jakob Burckhardt); Fritz Tegularius represents Nietzsche
Novel: the 'posthumous writings' include 'The Indian Life' (Dasa kills his half-brother Nala) and 'The Rainmaker' (Ada, daughter of Master Turu)
Novel: the protagonist travels to a bamboo grove and learns how to interpret bamboo from Elder Brother
Novel: the title character has 'the mark of Cain' and introduces Emil Sinclair to the Gnostic deity Abraxas
Novel: the title character stops the bully Franz Kromer from blackmailing the protagonist for lying about stealing apples
Novel: a boy appears to stop breathing during class, then passes a note: 'The bird is struggling out of the egg...whoever wants to be born must first destroy a world'
Novel: the protagonist is mentored by an organist named Pistorius and raised in a 'Scheinwelt'
Novel: the protagonist paints portraits of a beautiful girl that also turn out to depict the title character
Novel: one title character is sexually awakened by a gypsy, leaves the monastery, and apprentices under woodcarver Master Niklaus after seeing a beautiful Madonna
Novel: one title character appears immune to the Black Death during his years of wandering
Novel: the wandering title character carves his mother before dying; the other title character becomes abbot of Mariabronn
Novel: the wanderer becomes involved with a countess named Agnes, who stops helping him when he loses his beauty
Novel: 'The League' is a secret society including Mozart, Plato, Puss in Boots, and Paul Klee; the journey falls apart when Leo disappears at the gorge Morbio Inferiore
Novel: narrated by 'H.H.'; characters from other novels by the same author (Vasudeva, Klingsor) reappear as League members
Novel: a painter experiences a final burst of creativity in Italy; paints a self-portrait he locks in an unused kitchen
Novel: the title painter meets an Armenian astrologer who believes sadness can be permanently overcome in one hour of concentration
Novel: Hans Giebenrath is crushed by the German educational system; the title phrase comes from the Ephorus's warning
Novel: the title character leaves a mountain village to attend university and falls in love with painter Erminia; contrasts Nietzsche's ethics with Saint Francis of Assisi
Author: Hugo Ball (poet of 'Karawane,' author of the Dada Manifesto) wrote a 1927 biography of this author
